ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for mushroom-based XO-style sauce. Mapped to the nearest parent class 'condiment sauce (food product)' as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Sauces and similar products) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for mushroom-based XO-style condiment sauces in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Five-Spice XO Mushroom Sauce is approximately 15 grams (about one tablespoon), used as a condiment or flavour enhancer over noodles, congee, or steamed tofu.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mushroom-based sauce, composite estimate; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for shiitake mushrooms (FDC ID 168436), vegetable oil (FDC ID 171413), and garlic (FDC ID 169230)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Five-Spice XO Mushroom Sauce exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values were estimated by compositional analogy using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the primary ingredients (dried shiitake mushrooms, vegetable oil, garlic, ginger, chili) weighted by approximate recipe proportions. The input AI-generated values were reviewed against these USDA component estimates and found to be broadly consistent. Input values are retained with medium confidence. Sodium is high (1250 mg/100 g), consistent with a concentrated condiment sauce seasoned with soy sauce and salt. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Condiment Sauces — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate oil-and-mushroom-based sauce; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Five-Spice XO Mushroom Sauce. GI is estimated at 25 (low) by analogy with low-carbohydrate, high-fat, high-fibre condiment sauces. The sauce contains only 12.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which 4.5 g is dietary fibre, and is dominated by fat (38.5 g) and mushroom-derived fibre. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (25 × 1.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.8 g, classified as very low. Estimation basis is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database principles for high-fat, high-fibre mixed condiments.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (1250 mg per 100 g); a single 15 g serving contributes approximately 188 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit serving size to no more than one teaspoon (approximately 5–8 g) per meal, as the concentrated sodium load from soy sauce and salt seasoning can meaningfully contribute to daily sodium intake even in small portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 25) and low available carbohydrate per serving (approximately 1.25 g per 15 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Five-Spice XO Mushroom Sauce has a negligible glycemic impact per typical serving and can be used as a flavour enhancer by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided total caloric and fat intake is monitored given the high fat density of the sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (38.5 g/100 g) and high sodium (1250 mg/100 g); fat is predominantly from vegetable oil with a potentially high omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should use this sauce sparingly (no more than one tablespoon per meal) due to its high caloric density, elevated sodium content, and potential for high omega-6 vegetable oil contribution; choosing versions made with heart-healthier oils such as olive or avocado oil is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mushroom-derived polysaccharides and spices (ginger, Sichuan pepper) may have anti-inflammatory properties, but high omega-6 vegetable oil content may be pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Moderate use of this sauce may be acceptable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given the anti-inflammatory potential of its mushroom and spice components, but the omega-6-rich oil base should be considered in the context of overall dietary fat balance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Five-Spice XO Mushroom Sauce (五香菌姑XO酱 / 五香菌姑XO醬, Wǔxiāng jūngū XO jiàng) is a premium plant-based condiment that reimagines the classic Cantonese XO sauce by replacing dried seafood with a concentrated blend of high-umami mushrooms including shiitake, porcini, and king oyster varieties. The mushrooms are finely diced and slow-cooked in oil until crispy and deeply flavoured, then seasoned with Chinese five-spice powder (star anise, cloves, cinnamon, Sichuan pepper, and fennel seeds), garlic, ginger, chili, soy sauce, and a small amount of sugar. The result is a pungent, savoury, multi-layered condiment with a rich, chewy-crispy texture and intense umami depth. It is fully vegan and vegetarian. Nutritionally, it is calorie-dense (approximately 420 kcal per 100 g) and high in fat (38.5 g/100 g) due to the oil-infusion cooking method, with moderate protein (8.5 g/100 g) from mushrooms and significant dietary fibre (4.5 g/100 g). Sodium is very high (1250 mg/100 g), consistent with a concentrated condiment. It provides B-vitamins including niacin (4.5 mg), riboflavin (0.25 mg), and folate (35 mcg), as well as vitamin D (1.5 mcg) and selenium (12.5 mcg) from mushrooms. Vitamin B12 is absent as this is a fully plant-based product. The glycemic index is estimated at 25 (low), making it suitable for blood sugar management in typical serving sizes. Clinically, the primary concern is its high sodium content for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is used as a sophisticated topping for congee, noodles, silken tofu, and dumplings, and as a powerful flavour base for stir-fries and braises across Chinese contemporary cuisine.

topping for steamed or silken tofu
dipping sauce for dumplings and dim sum
flavour base for braises and slow-cooked dishes

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Five-Spice XO Mushroom Sauce exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values were estimated by compositional analogy using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries for the primary ingredients. The input AI-generated values were reviewed against these estimates and found broadly consistent. Medium confidence is assigned because the values are compositionally derived rather than directly measured from an approved source entry for this specific product.
notes: 1. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is 0.0: this is confirmed zero as the product is fully plant-based with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally absent in vegan foods unless fortified. Cholesterol (cholesterol_mg) is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a non-hydrogenated vegetable oil-based product. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this specific mushroom-based condiment sauce are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy vegetable oil component data. 3. GI value of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Estimation is based on the low available carbohydrate content, high fat, and high fibre profile of the sauce. 4. The input source (Zhejiang Academy of Agricultural Sciences) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence. USDA FoodData Central component estimates are used instead. 5. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 15 g entries) has been deduplicated to a single 15 g serving. 6. Ontology codes are approximate; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for mushroom-based XO-style condiment sauce.
